---
title: "Workflow: state BRFSS data"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Workflow: state BRFSS data}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment  = "#>",
  eval     = FALSE
)
```

This vignette walks through brfssTools end-to-end: from a fresh install
to a tidy tibble ready for analysis. It's written for analysts working
with state-collected BRFSS files — but the workflow is the same for
National-only users; just substitute `brfss_download()` for
`brfss_set_pool()` in step 5.

The code chunks are not executed at vignette build time. Run them
yourself against your real machine and your real data.

## 1. Install

```r
# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("nfparsons/brfssTools")
```

A fresh install ships with:

- The CDC codebook and taxonomy (`inst/extdata/`)
- A small DEMO state codebook with 15 fake variables (for trying the
  workflow without your real data)
- 19 demographic templates covering the BRFSS "big six" (age, sex, race,
  ethnicity, education, income)
- A CDC seed crosswalk: every CDC variable for every year 2012-2024,
  ready to be mapped to your state's columns

## 2. Initialize your config

brfssTools keeps user data — your crosswalk, transformations, state
codebook — separate from the package install, so it persists across
package upgrades. Initialize this once:

```r
library(brfssTools)

brfss_init_state(
  state               = "OR",
  state_codebook_path = "~/data/oregon_brfss_codebook.csv"
)
#> Initialized config for state 'OR' with seeded CDC crosswalk. All CDC
#> variables are present with state_var = NA and unverified = 1. Open
#> brfss_crosswalk_editor() to map your state codebook columns.
#> Config dir: C:/Users/.../AppData/Roaming/R/config/R/brfssTools
```

Two things just happened:

1. Your state codebook was validated and copied into the config dir
2. The crosswalk was seeded with all 4,209 CDC variables across 2012-2024,
   each marked `unverified = 1` and waiting for you to fill in `state_var`

If you don't have your real state codebook handy yet, try the demo:

```r
brfss_init_state("DEMO")
```

The DEMO ships with 15 fake variables prefixed `EXAMPLE_*` so it's
unmistakably non-real.

For project-local config (so you can git-track it alongside an analysis):

```r
brfss_init_state(
  state               = "OR",
  state_codebook_path = "~/data/oregon_brfss_codebook.csv",
  path                = "~/projects/aces-paper/brfss_config"
)
```

## 3. See where everything lives

```r
brfss_status()
#>
#> brfssTools status
#> -----------------
#>
#> Config directory:
#>   C:/Users/.../AppData/Roaming/R/config/R/brfssTools
#>   state:       OR
#>   initialized: 2026-04-30 14:31:23 PDT (user_codebook)
#>
#> Config files:
#>   crosswalk          4209 rows  (161.1 KB)
#>   state_codebook     5120 rows  (412.8 KB)
#>   ...
#>
#> Transformations:
#>   (none)
#>
#> Registered data pools:
#>   (none registered)
#>   Use brfss_set_pool() or brfss_download() to register.
```

Run this any time you're not sure where the package is looking. The
output also tells you what to do next based on what's missing.

## 4. Set up your demographics

This step comes BEFORE outcome-variable mapping. Demographics are
prerequisites — most analyses need a working `age`, `sex`, and `race`
before any outcome makes sense.

brfssTools ships templates for the BRFSS big six. List them:

```r
brfss_list_demographic_templates()
#> # A tibble: 19 × 4
#>    demographic  template               description                     path
#>    <chr>        <chr>                  <chr>                           <chr>
#>  1 age          binary_65plus          Binary age: under 65, 65+       ...
#>  2 age          cdc_5year              5-year groups, 13 categories    ...
#>  3 age          cdc_6group             CDC standard 6-group adult age  ...
#>  4 age          continuous             Continuous age, passthrough     ...
#>  5 age          samhsa                 SAMHSA / NSDUH age groupings    ...
#>  6 age          suicide_surveillance   Suicide surveillance groupings  ...
#>  7 education    cdc_4category          CDC 4-category education        ...
#>  8 education    cdc_6category          CDC 6-category education        ...
#>  9 ethnicity    hispanic_binary        Binary Hispanic ethnicity       ...
#> 10 ethnicity    hispanic_subgroups     Hispanic with subgroups         ...
#> 11 income       cdc_2021plus           Post-2021 INCOME3 brackets      ...
#> 12 income       cdc_pre2021            Pre-2021 INCOME2 brackets       ...
#> 13 income       cdc_unified            Year-aware unified income       ...
#> 14 income       quartiles              4 broad bands, cross-year       ...
#> 15 race         cdc_9category          CDC _RACE 9-category            ...
#> 16 race         omb_5category          OMB 5-category collapsed        ...
#> 17 sex          birth_binary           Sex assigned at birth, binary   ...
#> 18 sex          gender_identity_4cat   Gender identity 4-category      ...
#> 19 sex          selfreport_binary      Self-reported sex, binary       ...
```

Set up each demographic. Each call copies a template into your config
dir's `transformations/` folder and opens it in your editor for review:

```r
brfss_setup_demographic("age",       template = "cdc_6group")
brfss_setup_demographic("sex",       template = "selfreport_binary")
brfss_setup_demographic("race",      template = "cdc_9category")
brfss_setup_demographic("ethnicity", template = "hispanic_binary")
brfss_setup_demographic("education", template = "cdc_4category")
brfss_setup_demographic("income",    template = "cdc_unified")
```

Each template targets CDC variable names by default (`_AGE80`, `SEXVAR`,
`_MRACE1`, `_HISPANC`, `EDUCA`, `INCOME3`, etc.). **If your state codebook
uses different column names, edit the YAML's `inputs:` block to point
at your codebook's columns.**

For variables whose column name changes year-to-year (Oregon's `MRACE1_*`
2017-2021 → `MRACE2_*` 2022-2024 is a classic example), use a `by_year:`
block. The shipped templates include commented-out examples.

You can also choose to skip the GUI editor here and use the
**Demographics tab** in the crosswalk editor (see step 6) to manage
demographics interactively.

Confirm everything is set up:

```r
brfss_demographic_status()
#> # A tibble: 6 × 4
#>   demographic status      kind  path
#>   <chr>       <chr>       <chr> <chr>
#> 1 age         configured  yaml  .../transformations/age.yaml
#> 2 sex         configured  yaml  .../transformations/sex.yaml
#> 3 race        configured  yaml  .../transformations/race.yaml
#> 4 ethnicity   configured  yaml  .../transformations/ethnicity.yaml
#> 5 education   configured  yaml  .../transformations/education.yaml
#> 6 income      configured  yaml  .../transformations/income.yaml
```

## 5. Register your raw data files

Point the package at your state's data directory. Files can be `.csv`
or `.xpt`; year is auto-detected from filename or the `SEQNO` column.

```r
brfss_set_pool("OR", "Z:/Secure_Data/Oregon_BRFSS")
#> Scanning pool for dataset 'OR'...
#> Indexed 13 file(s) for dataset 'OR'.

brfss_pool_status()
#> # A tibble: 13 × 3
#>    dataset  year  file
#>  ...
```

The pool registration is session-scoped (re-run on each R session).
Most analysts wrap this in a `.Rprofile` or project setup script.

For National data, use `brfss_download()` instead — it fetches CDC's
LLCP files and auto-registers them as the "National" pool.

## 6. Map outcome variables in the editor

Now you map the outcome variables your analysis cares about.
Demographics are already done (step 4) — the editor is for everything
else.

```r
brfss_crosswalk_editor()
```

Three views (radio buttons at the top):

- **CDC-anchored** (default) — every CDC variable as a row, your state's
  mappings shown as columns. Best for "I want this CDC variable; which
  state column do I use?"
- **State-anchored** — every state variable as a row, CDC matches as
  columns. Best for "what does my state collect?"
- **Demographics** — checklist of the big six with template picker.
  Same workflow as `brfss_setup_demographic()` but in the GUI.

In CDC-anchored or State-anchored view:

1. Click a row in the heatmap to open the right-side edit panel
2. For seeded rows (where `state_var = NA`), assign your state column
   from the codebook
3. **Verify** the row when you've confirmed the mapping is correct
   (clears the `unverified` flag)

The editor saves to your config dir; nothing is committed until you
click **Save** in the header. Each save creates a `.bak` rotation.

For partial work — map the variables you need today, leave the rest
seeded for later. The crosswalk is yours to build incrementally.

## 7. Pull data

```r
df <- brfss_pull(
  concepts = c("ACEADNED", "GENHLTH"),
  years    = 2019:2024
)
```

Things that happen automatically:

- `age`, `sex`, `race` are pulled (because they're configured demographics
  and `core_demographics = TRUE` is the default)
- Survey design variables (`.weight`, `.strata`, `.psu`) come along
- For each year, the right state column is resolved from the crosswalk
- Categorical_map and passthrough transformations run per-year, with
  `by_year` overrides honored

```r
df
#> # A tibble: ~120,000 × 9
#>     year SEQNO       `_STATE` ACEADNED GENHLTH age   sex   race  .weight
#>    <int> <chr>       <chr>    <chr>    <chr>   <chr> <chr> <chr>   <dbl>
#>  ...
```

Other patterns:

```r
# Pull every concept in a domain:
df <- brfss_pull(domains = "Behavioral Health", years = 2022:2024)

# Long format (one row per respondent × concept):
df <- brfss_pull(
  concepts = c("ACEADNED", "GENHLTH"),
  years    = 2022,
  output   = "long"
)

# Skip auto-included demographics:
df <- brfss_pull(
  concepts          = "ACEADNED",
  years             = 2024,
  core_demographics = FALSE
)
```

## 8. From here, your usual tidyverse workflow takes over

`brfss_pull()` returns a tibble. Survey-design columns are named
`.weight`, `.strata`, `.psu` so they don't collide with anything else.

From here, it's any tidyverse-compatible package: `dplyr`/`tidyr` for
reshaping, `srvyr` or `survey` for weighted estimates, `ggplot2` for
visualization.

## Sharing your work

Your crosswalk lives in your user config dir. To move it to another
machine, archive it, or share with a collaborator:

```r
brfss_export_config("~/oregon_crosswalk_2026-04-30.zip")
# On the destination machine:
brfss_import_config("~/oregon_crosswalk_2026-04-30.zip")
```

To start over (with automatic backup):

```r
brfss_reset()
# Backs up the current config to a timestamped folder, reinits to DEMO.
```

## Troubleshooting

**`No pool registered for dataset 'OR'`** — re-run `brfss_set_pool()`.
Pool registration is session-scoped.

**Transformation produces all NAs** — the YAML's `inputs:` references
columns that don't exist for that year. Edit the YAML or add a
`by_year:` block.

**Editor crashes on a row** — try `cw_load() |> str()` to inspect the
crosswalk; the issue may be a row whose schema is malformed. Last
resort: `brfss_reset()` and reimport from a recent export.

**`brfss_status()` shows "missing" for a file** — the file isn't in
your config dir. Run `brfss_init_state(...)` again with `overwrite = TRUE`
or restore from a backup.
